original_abstract: The paradigm shift introduced by Law Number 11 of 2012 concerning the Juvenile Justice System—specifically regarding restorative justice and diversion—demands a high level of specialized competency and skill from law enforcement officials. This research examines the measures taken and the structural barriers encountered during the preparatory phase of the law's implementation. The findings reveal a significant gap in institutional readiness: while investigators and juvenile prosecutors have primarily engaged with the law through basic outreach programs, the judicial officers’ knowledge often remains limited to the preliminary draft stages. Furthermore, the volume of personnel who have received adequate training is insufficient and requires immediate scaling. Beyond technical expertise, the study highlights a critical psychological barrier; there is a pervasive fear among officers that exercising the discretion required for diversion could be misinterpreted as bribery. This negative perception risks stifling the effective application of restorative justice practices in the field.
humanized_abstract: This paper discussed the capacity building needed from law enforcement officials to comply with Law Number 11 of 2012 regarding the juvenile justice system using restorative justice and diversion. In this paper, the authors dealt with the efforts made and obstacles encountered in the preparatory stage of implementing this law. The results showed that in general law enforcement officials – including investigators and juvenile prosecutors – only went through a socialization effort in a very primary level. Law enforcement officials at the judicial level also barely had more than a socialization that was developed based on the drafts of the law. It was found that the number of people who went through more in-depth training in preparation of the law was not satisfactory and needs to be addressed immediately. In addition to the law enforcement’s skills needed to implement Law No. 11 of 2012, another challenge that appears more deep-seated is psychological; it stems from law enforcement officers’ deep-seated fear that having mercy towards minors and exercising their discretional power to apply the juvenile justice system would give rise to societal belief that it is tantamount to being corrupted through bribery.
